title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Water
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Nation-state actors keep breaching water utilities through internet-exposed PLCs — no malware required
items.content_text: Fresh threat-intelligence reporting warns that state actors from Iran, Russia and China are repeatedly breaking into water and wastewater utilities not with novel malware but by walking through internet-exposed PLCs, HMIs and unsegmented remote access secured with default or shared credentials. Canada's Communications Security Establishment disclosed that a pro-Russia hacktivist group claimed it breached a Quebec municipal water plant and could covertly manipulate chlorine dosing and pump pressure — a claim CSE has not independently verified. Whether or not that specific boast holds, the pattern is now a deliberate feature of state competition, and water's thin staffing and flat networks make it the softest target in critical infrastructure. The fix is unglamorous: get controllers off the public internet, kill default credentials, segment OT from IT, and alarm on setpoint changes.

items.title: China-linked CL-STA-1062 hits water and electricity utilities with the new TinyRCT backdoor
items.content_text: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 reported in late June that a Chinese-speaking group, CL-STA-1062, compromised water and electricity utilities plus government and military targets across Southeast Asia, planting a lightweight backdoor called TinyRCT for persistence, command execution and stealthy exfiltration, complete with a self-destruct to erase evidence. For water operators it is a reminder that nation-state pre-positioning starts in ordinary business IT — billing, historians, remote-access jump hosts — then looks for a path into treatment OT. Hunt for quiet persistence and prove your IT compromise cannot reach the plant.

items.title: FortiBleed credential theft now feeds ransomware — the VPN into your SCADA is the exposure
items.content_text: SOCRadar reported on 2 July that the FortiBleed campaign — a sniffer harvesting 110M+ credentials from some 430,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls since mid-June — is now driving INC Ransom and Lynx deployments. Many water and wastewater utilities front their remote SCADA access and vendor connections with exactly these appliances. Because credentials leaked rather than a bug being exploited, there is no patch to apply: an attacker logs in as a trusted operator. Assume any exposed FortiGate touching your OT network is compromised and rotate now.

items.title: Iran's CyberAv3ngers keep targeting water-sector PLCs — default credentials remain the way in
items.content_text: The April joint advisory AA26-097A confirmed Iranian IRGC-linked actors, operating as CyberAv3ngers, are actively reaching Rockwell and Unitronics programmable logic controllers at U.S. water plants using default or weak factory credentials — the same playbook behind the 2023 Aliquippa incident. The campaign is ongoing and agency-verified, not a one-off. Small utilities that expose Unitronics or Vision-series HMIs to the internet with unchanged defaults are the prime targets; the countermeasure is to remove that exposure and replace factory passwords everywhere.

items.title: Living-off-the-land is reshaping OT incident response — and engineering-led defense is the answer
items.content_text: Analysts report that adversaries in industrial environments increasingly avoid custom malware in favor of legitimate tools and valid accounts, making intrusions blend into normal operations and defeating signature-based detection. For water utilities, where fewer than one in ten OT networks has any monitoring, that means an attacker manipulating a dosing setpoint may look identical to a technician. The recommended shift is engineering-led: operators who know what normal process behavior looks like become the detection layer, backed by OT-aware monitoring and validated safe-state controls.

items.title: iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals flagged — the backhaul at remote lift stations and tank sites
items.content_text: CISA's 2 July advisory (ICSA-26-183-01) covers ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals, with two API flaws allowing an unauthenticated attacker to read device details (CVE-2026-38059) or force reboots that sever the link (CVE-2026-38057). Water and wastewater systems use satellite backhaul at remote pump stations, tanks and outfalls where fiber and cellular do not reach, so a vulnerable terminal is both a foothold and a denial-of-communications risk at unmanned sites. iDirect has released fixed software 4.5.2.2.

items.title: June CISA advisory cycle flags Schneider and Mitsubishi gear used in water treatment control
items.content_text: A June CISA advisory cycle covered vulnerabilities across Schneider Electric, Mitsubishi Electric, Delta and others — controllers and modules widely deployed in water and wastewater treatment for pumping, dosing and SCADA. Most items ship with vendor fixes or mitigations. For utilities the discipline is to map the flagged products against your plant inventory and prioritize patching anything internet-reachable or tied to chemical dosing and pumping, where an integrity failure has public-health consequences.

items.title: CISA and EPA keep pressing water systems to pull HMIs off the public internet
items.content_text: CISA and EPA guidance continues to warn that internet-exposed human-machine interfaces let attackers view and alter treatment operations directly — the exact vector pro-Russia hacktivists used to max out setpoints and lock out operators at U.S. utilities. The remediation is foundational and unchanged: remove HMIs from public exposure, put remote access behind a VPN with MFA, and inventory what is reachable from the internet. It remains the single highest-leverage step a small utility can take.

items.title: EPA cybersecurity resources give small water systems a starting playbook
items.content_text: EPA's water-sector cybersecurity program offers free planning tools, an incident-response plan template and assessment guidance aimed squarely at the small and mid-size utilities that make up most of the sector and have the least security staff. With reported cyber incidents against water utilities up sharply this decade, these resources are a pragmatic on-ramp: a documented IR plan, a baseline assessment and a procurement checklist for vendor security are achievable even without a dedicated OT security team.
